item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, simple, poignant. I didn't much care for the addendum, but it's not enough to negate the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: but, of course, there's no way to know. The fossil record is fragmentary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was working under the idea that they came from our future

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Were those unidentified elephants from the past, but left behind little to no fossil record? Or did they come from our future?

I had to ask, I'm a stickler for backstory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was certainly interesting! I appreciate the brevity, for all that you take on quite the trip. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the brevity, and the last log and ending left me satisfied with this reading of a temporal elephant graveyard. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Elephants crying over a dead elephant is both a real phenomenon, and cool imagery. The way the agent reacts to touching the elephant bones feels sort of forced. Having him return home as an elephant is weird though, and good. I dunno. Nothing much to hold my attention, so I'm gonna have to downvote.

EDIT: I find it odd that I can find more reasons to like this than to dislike it, but I guess it doesn't resonate with me? I dunno, might change that to a novote, seeing as I can't put my finger on what's really making me downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, my third skip. Thanks to Typhos, Tygently, shaggy, maxson, icepick, drbleep and everyone else who took a look at this to get it where it is now

Image is from Wikimedia https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:IndianElephant.jpg
